writing_type: nonprofit donor newsletters and fundraising communications
user_goals_summary: Writes donor communications that center community members as protagonists with full agency, position donors as strategic investors whose decisions produced specific outcomes, and build toward asks that feel like logical continuations of an existing strategy rather than appeals to generosity. Prioritizes narrative craft — story-first sequencing, active verbs, named individuals carrying through the full piece — as the mechanism for earning both emotional connection and financial commitment.

rubric.name: Community-Centered Voice
rubric.description: Community members — not the organization — are the grammatical subjects and protagonists of the piece. The organization is present in the implied infrastructure but does not narrate its own work. Beneficiaries are shown choosing, deciding, and acting, never positioned as passive recipients.

dimensions.label: Community members (individuals or families) are the grammatical subjects of most key sentences
Beneficiaries are shown exercising agency — choosing, deciding, walking toward — rather than receiving services
The organization rarely appears as a grammatical subject; its role is implied through infrastructure, not narrated as achievement
The piece avoids 'look what we accomplished' framing in favor of 'here is what is happening in these communities'

rubric.name: Strategic Donor Framing
rubric.description: Donors are positioned as effective strategic investors whose past decisions produced specific measurable outcomes, not as generous benefactors being thanked. Language borrows from strategy and finance — 'compounds,' 'logical next move,' 'calculus' — to activate the donor's identity as someone who makes smart calls, not someone who gives charitably.

dimensions.label: Past giving is credited with producing a specific, named outcome rather than appreciated abstractly
Uses strategic or financial vocabulary (e.g., 'compounds,' 'calculus,' 'logical next move') to speak to the donor's investor identity
Donor is made to feel effective and smart, not generous and appreciated
The ask is framed as a continuation of an existing strategy, not a new request for help

rubric.name: Story-First Evidence Integration
rubric.description: Human narrative always precedes data. Numbers arrive as confirmation of what the reader already feels, not as the opening argument. When data reappears later in the piece, it callbacks to the narrative that earned it, anchoring the evidence to the emotional through-line.

dimensions.label: A human image or story is established before any impact number is introduced
Data points land as confirmation of the story's significance rather than as a standalone pitch for impact
When numbers reappear in later sections, they are tied back to the narrative context that originally earned them
Scale emerges from individual stories outward (specific person → broader cohort) rather than from aggregate statistics downward

rubric.name: Protagonist Continuity
rubric.description: Named individuals introduced early in the piece remain present and active throughout, especially in the closing. The protagonist's arc must arrive somewhere — callbacks, echoes, and parallel structure connect opening images to closing images so the emotional through-line doesn't fade at the moment it matters most.

dimensions.label: Named individuals from the opening reappear by name in or near the closing section
The protagonist's story has a discernible arc — it arrives somewhere rather than simply being introduced and dropped
Closing images deliberately echo or mirror opening images (parallel actions, callbacks, repeated motifs)
The final emotional image is a specific named person doing something, not a generalized outcome

rubric.name: Earned and Specific Ask
rubric.description: The call to action grows organically from the narrative rather than being appended as a fundraising block. It is specific and bounded — a named dollar amount, a defined number of units, a clear deadline — and connects directly to the protagonist's story so the donor sees the gift as the next chapter, not a separate transaction.

dimensions.label: The ask emerges from the narrative as a logical next step rather than arriving as a tacked-on appeal
The ask includes a specific dollar amount, a defined number of units or people, and/or a clear timeframe
The ask connects directly back to the named protagonist's story or situation
The close creates forward momentum and invitation rather than gratitude and closure

rubric.name: Active, Concrete Language
rubric.description: Verbs show motion and action rather than states and readiness. At key emotional moments — especially openings and closings — language is concrete and imagistic rather than abstract. The reader should be able to picture someone doing something, not just understand that something is the case.

dimensions.label: Key sentences use verbs of action and motion (walked, walking, stepped) rather than states (is ready, are able, are at the threshold)
Emotional high points use specific, visual imagery rather than abstract or generalized language
Descriptions reference particular people, places, and moments rather than categories or types
Verb choices are deliberate and precise — each verb earns its place and carries the intended energy (active, volitional, forward)

coaching_notes: This user has an exceptionally precise editorial eye — they will iterate multiple rounds on single verb choices and sentence-level craft decisions, and those micro-revisions are where the real work happens. Non-negotiables: community members as protagonists with agency, donors framed as strategic investors (never as charitable helpers), and story always earning the data. When the user says 'don't touch that line,' it is locked — build around it. The user thinks in terms of donor psychology and reading position: they evaluate every sentence by asking 'how does the donor feel about themselves while reading this?'

rubric.name: Strategic Donor Framing
rubric.description: Donors are positioned as effective strategic investors whose past decisions produced specific measurable outcomes, not as generous benefactors being thanked. Language borrows from strategy and finance — 'compounds,' 'logical next move,' 'calculus' — to activate the donor's identity as someone who makes smart calls, not someone who gives charitably. The organization must not appear as the hero or actor of the outcomes; framing the nonprofit as the agent ('we ran,' 'we built,' 'your gift helped us') collapses the donor's strategic identity into a supporting role and must be avoided.

dimensions.label: Past giving is credited with producing a specific, named outcome rather than appreciated abstractly

The ask is framed as a continuation of an existing strategy, not a new request for help

The organization is not positioned as the actor driving outcomes — phrases like 'your gift helped us' or 'we ran this program' are avoided because they make the nonprofit the hero and reduce the donor to a funder

user_goals_summary: Writes donor-facing pieces that center the people served as active protagonists — not the organization — while positioning donors as strategic investors whose past decisions produced measurable results. Values a story-first architecture where named individuals carry the emotional through-line from opening to closing, and where the ask emerges organically as the logical next chapter of the protagonist's story.

rubric.name: Community-Centered Voice
rubric.description: The people served — not the organization — are the grammatical subjects and protagonists of the piece. The organization is present in implied infrastructure but does not narrate its own work or claim credit for capacity. Individuals are shown choosing, deciding, and acting, never positioned as passive recipients of services.

dimensions.label: Individuals served (named people, families, cohorts) are the grammatical subjects of most key sentences
People served are shown exercising agency — choosing, deciding, walking toward, betting on themselves — rather than receiving interventions
The organization rarely appears as a grammatical subject; infrastructure reads as existing conditions ('a facility is confirmed') rather than organizational achievements ('we secured a facility')
The piece avoids 'look what we accomplished' framing in favor of describing what is happening in communities and in people's lives

rubric.name: Protagonist Continuity
rubric.description: Named individuals introduced early remain present and active throughout the piece, especially in the closing. The protagonist's arc must arrive somewhere — callbacks, echoes, and parallel structure connect opening images to closing images. The final emotional image is the named protagonist in motion, not a generalized outcome or abstracted beneficiary.

dimensions.label: Named individuals from the opening reappear by name in or near the closing section
The protagonist's story has a discernible arc that arrives somewhere rather than being introduced and dropped
Closing images deliberately echo or mirror opening images through parallel actions, callbacks, or repeated motifs
The final emotional image is a specific named person doing something concrete, not a generalized outcome like 'a girl who stays enrolled'
The named protagonist is present and active in the paragraph where the donor ask lands, not just in the setup

rubric.name: Strategic Donor Framing
rubric.description: Donors are positioned as effective strategic investors whose past decisions produced specific measurable outcomes, not as generous benefactors being thanked. Language borrows from strategy and finance — 'compounds,' 'logical next move,' 'calculus' — to activate the donor's identity as someone who makes smart calls. The ask reads as the next move in an existing strategy, not a new request for help.

dimensions.label: Past giving is credited with producing a specific, named outcome rather than appreciated abstractly
Uses strategic or financial vocabulary (e.g., 'compounds,' 'calculus,' 'logical next move,' 'proof') to speak to the donor's investor identity
Donor is made to feel effective and smart rather than generous and appreciated — like someone who ran an experiment that returned results
The ask is framed as a continuation or compounding of an existing strategy, not a new request for help

The organization is not positioned as the agent driving outcomes — phrasings like 'your gift helped us' or 'we ran this program' are avoided because they make the nonprofit the hero and reduce the donor to a funder

rubric.name: Story-First Evidence Integration

Scale is expressed by multiplying the named protagonist's outcome outward — the individual becomes the unit of measurement — so the reader is multiplying a known story, not processing an abstraction.

dimensions.label: The ask emerges from the narrative as a logical next step rather than arriving as a tacked-on appeal

The ask connects directly back to the named protagonist's story, situation, or cohort
The close creates forward momentum and invitation — the story is unfinished in the reader's mind, resolvable through continued investment — rather than gratitude and closure

rubric.name: Active, Concrete Language
rubric.description: Verbs show motion and action rather than states and readiness. At key emotional moments — especially openings and closings — language is concrete and imagistic rather than abstract. dimensions.label: Key sentences use verbs of action and motion (walked, walking, stepped) rather than states (is ready, are able, are at the threshold)
Emotional high points use specific, visual imagery rather than abstract or generalized language
Descriptions reference particular people, places, and moments rather than categories or types
Verb choices are deliberate and precise — each verb earns its place and carries the intended energy (active, volitional, forward)

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: named protagonists must be present and active in the closing paragraph (the most frequently revised element across all drafts); the organization must never be the grammatical hero; donor framing lines like 'logical next move' and 'if the first decision was sound' represent the exact register this user wants — strategic, confident, slightly presumptuous. Key trade-off: this user will always sacrifice organizational credit for protagonist agency and donor empowerment — when in doubt, make the person served the subject and the donor the strategist. Feedback pattern: this user gives highly specific, craft-level revision notes — often a single verb or sentence-level fix — and iterates patiently toward precision rather than requesting wholesale rewrites.

dimensions.label: A human image or story is established before any impact number is introduced
Data points land as confirmation of the story's significance rather than as a standalone pitch for impact

Scale is expressed by first building a human image of people in the protagonist's position, then letting the count arrive as confirmation of that image — the reader feels the number before seeing it

rubric.name: Active, Concrete Language
rubric.description: Verbs show motion and action rather than states and readiness. At key emotional moments — especially openings and closings — language is concrete and imagistic rather than abstract. The reader should be able to picture someone doing something, not just understand that something is the case.

dimensions.label: Key sentences use verbs of action and motion (walked, walking, negotiated) rather than states (is ready, are able, are at the threshold)
Emotional high points use specific, visual imagery rather than abstract or generalized language
Descriptions reference particular people, places, and moments rather than categories or types
Verb choices are deliberate and precise — each verb earns its place and carries the intended energy (active, volitional, forward)

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: the organization must never be the hero, the protagonist must be present and active at the close, and the donor must feel effective rather than thanked. This user's revision pattern is consistent across pieces: they accept a strong opening quickly, then spend multiple rounds refining the closing — especially protagonist continuity through the ask and the precise emotional register of the donor framing. When choosing between warmth and strategic precision, this user will always choose precision — 'the calculus changes' over 'we're grateful for your support.' Trust their instinct that a subtle verb shift or subject change matters; they are editing at the sentence-grammar level for a reason.

coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is the causal arrow: funding creates access, people create outcomes. Any sentence that implies the program or donation 'produced' someone's success will be flagged and rejected, even when the rest of the piece is strong. The user thinks in terms of grammatical subjects as a proxy for values — who holds the verb tells the reader who holds the power. When the user says 'the org is doing too much of the moving,' the fix is almost always a subject-swap, not a content change. The user's preferred trade-off: they will sacrifice warmth and gratitude for strategic precision every time — 'feel smart, not virtuous' is their north star for donor voice.

Data points land as confirmation of the story's significance rather than as a standalone pitch for impact
When numbers reappear in later sections, they are tied back to the narrative context that originally earned them
Scale is expressed by first building a human image of people in the protagonist's position, then letting the count arrive as confirmation of that image — the reader feels the number before seeing it

rubric.name: Earned and Specific Ask
rubric.description: The call to action grows organically from the narrative rather than being appended as a fundraising block. It is specific and bounded — a named dollar amount, a defined number of units, a clear deadline — and connects directly to the protagonist's story so the donor sees the gift as the next chapter of a known outcome, not a separate transaction.

dimensions.label: The ask emerges from the narrative as a logical next step rather than arriving as a tacked-on appeal
The ask includes a specific dollar amount, a defined number of units or people, and/or a clear timeframe
The ask connects directly back to the named protagonist's story, situation, or cohort — the gift is framed in protagonist-sized units
The close creates forward momentum and invitation — the story is unfinished in the reader's mind, resolvable through continued investment — rather than gratitude and closure

rubric.name: Active, Concrete Language
rubric.description: Verbs show motion and action rather than states and readiness. At key emotional moments — especially openings and closings — language is concrete and imagistic rather than abstract. The reader should be able to picture someone doing something specific, not just understand that something is the case. Declarative, punchy constructions are preferred over hedged or elaborate ones.

dimensions.label: Key sentences use verbs of action and motion (walked, walking, negotiated) rather than states (is ready, are able, are at the threshold)
Emotional high points use specific, visual imagery rather than abstract or generalized language
Descriptions reference particular people, places, and moments rather than categories or types
Verb choices are deliberate and precise — each verb earns its place and carries the intended energy (active, volitional, forward)

rubric.name: Strategic Donor Framing
rubric.description: Donors are positioned as effective strategic analysts whose past judgment produced specific measurable outcomes — the goal is to make them feel smart, not virtuous. Language borrows from strategy and finance ('compounds,' 'logical next move,' 'calculus,' 'calculated bet') to activate the donor's investor identity. The ask reads as the next move in an existing strategy, not a new appeal. Strategic framing must stay anchored to the named protagonist's arc, not float as program-level abstraction.

dimensions.label: The donor's past decision is framed as an analytical judgment that produced specific evidence — crediting their decision-making quality, not just their dollars or generosity
Uses strategic or financial vocabulary (e.g., 'compounds,' 'calculus,' 'logical next move,' 'proof,' 'calculated bet') to speak to the donor's investor identity
The ask is framed as a continuation or compounding of an existing strategy, not a new request for help
The piece avoids guilt-based, obligation-based, or desperate framing — the tone is one of strategic respect, not emotional manipulation or pleading
Strategic and ROI language is tied to the named protagonist's specific arc — investor framing that floats free of the individual story reads as abstraction, not evidence

user_goals_summary: Writes donor-facing fundraising appeals, newsletters, and impact reports that center the agency of the people served — not the organization — while positioning donors as strategic investors whose judgment produced specific outcomes. Every piece anchors around a named protagonist whose story earns the data, earns the ask, and carries through to the close.
coaching_notes: This user's non-negotiable is the causal arrow: funding creates access, people create outcomes. Any sentence that implies the program or donation 'produced' someone's success will be flagged and rejected, even when the rest of the piece is strong. The user thinks in terms of grammatical subjects as a proxy for values — who holds the verb tells the reader who holds the power. When the user says 'the org is doing too much of the moving,' the fix is almost always a subject-swap, not a content change. The user's preferred trade-off: they will sacrifice warmth and gratitude for strategic precision every time — 'feel smart, not virtuous' is their north star for donor voice.
